# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Need some alien sci-fi movie recs I haven’t seen yet

**Score:** 29
**Body:** I love sci-fi alien movies but feel like I’ve already watched all the mainstream ones. Can anyone recommend some *underrated*, *hidden gems*, or *weird but good* alien films?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. District 9 if you haven’5 seen that one.
2. Starman
3. The Vast of Night

No One Will Save You

The Fourth Kind
4. Galaxy Quest (1999)
5. Under the Skin
6. The Last Starfighter   1984

A little dated but it has a good heart.
7. Dark Star. Not completely alien, but there is one in it
8. Attack the block. Really fun one.
9. V (1983). Used to scare the shit out of me haha.
10. Color Out of Space (2019) - go in blind, it’s great!

## 2. Best films under 80 min?

**Score:** 24
**Body:** Just watched Run Lola Run and Bride of Frankenstein which both were amazing. Any other recommendations for films under 80 mins? Any genre as long as it’s good 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Detour - 1945
2. [REC] clocks in at about 76 minutes and the pacing is top-notch. Not a moment wasted or overdone.
3. Primer (2004)
4. Bananas is 82. Kentucky Fried Movie is 83.  Sleeper is 89. The Groove Tube is  73 and the only one I can find under 80.
5. Passion of Joan of Arc (82 min)

Fantastic Planet (72 min)

Frankenstein (70 min)

Fruitvale Station (82 min)

Corpse Bride (77 min)

Clean, Shaven (79 min)

The Squid and The Wale (81 min)
  - Fantastic Planet w a r p e d my mind
  - I misread Clean, Shaven as Clean Shaven. Totally different plot.
6. Pickpocket
7. Cat people (1942)
8. Before Sunset (2004) is 80 minutes on the dot. 

If you're strict about shorter than that, I'd give Shiva Baby (2020)[78 min.], Emperor's New Groove (2000)[78 min.], and/or Beyond the Infinite Two Minutes (2020)[70 min.] a shot.

## 3. Drop some of your all time favorite feel-good movies you would watch during the holiday season

**Score:** 20
**Body:** Looking for some new set of feel-good movies you can just watch while relaxing on christmas eve. 

I know there are like a gazillion such lists but many of them are quite old and there are so many new movies to add. So go ahead, drop your best picks of movies you would watch on a cozy winter evening this holiday season. 



### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Ghost Buster (just the first one).

Groundhog Day. 

My Cousin Vinny. 

Harry Potter (the first one, not a HP fan) 

Yes Man! 

Airplane!

National Treasure (all of em) 

Night at the Museum (all of em)
  - I know a man of culture when I see one.
  - This is a great list.
2. It’s a Wonderful Life

A Christmas Carol (whichever version is your favorite. I personal like the 2009 version with Jim Carrey)
3. Palm Springs

Yesterday
4. Die Hard, Love Actually.
5. Love Actually

The Holiday

The Holdovers

Bad Santa

The Family Stone

Little Women (1994)
6.  Four Christmases, Bad Moms Christmas, of course Christmas Vacation, all
7. The family man

While you were sleeping 

12 dates of Christmas 

The family stone
8. Conan the Barbarian 1982

Rocky

## 4. The Lost Boys (1987)

**Score:** 18
**Body:** This is one of the best vmpre-themed movies that I have seen. Though it aint fun to be one. 😁
(re poster's tagline) This is from the 80's, and the lead actor was so handsome n here. I feel like watching a vmpre-themed tonight. Any movie reco that is worth the watch?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Near dark.
  - Near dark goes hard the vibe is gritty and cool and it hits that same stylish vampire feel so it’s a solid pick
  - It's lickin' good!
  - The bar scene with Bill Paxton is perfect.
    - I wanna do a rewatch.
2. Fright Night and Near Dark the 2 other great vampire films of the 80’s.
  - Yes. Agreed. Makes me wanna watch Fright Night or Near Dark again! 😃 hehe Either tonight or this weekend. Will check the other suggestion'
3. Vampires.... James Wood is great

Edit: my fave has to be "Innocent Blood"

Anne Parillud (sp?) is sexy as hell lol
  - Innocent Blood is awesome.
4. Interview with a Vampire

## 5. Top movies from 2025

**Score:** 16
**Body:** Can someone help me out by shareing atleast 5 best movies you watched in 2025. Genre doesn't matter as long the movie is good. Would also love to hear from you  a detail from the movie that made you like it.

Movie language does not matter and actors are unimportant as long as the moviesis good. Thank you ✌️

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Ballad of Wallis Island is the best new movie I saw this year
2. - sentimental value
- if i had legs i'd kick you
- one battle after another
- sorry, baby
- twinless
- it was just an accident
  - I haven't seen all.pf those but really liked Sorry, Baby. I feel.like that movie doesn't get enough love!
3. Bugonia!
4. my top 10 goes One Battle After Another, Weapons, Sinners, Eddington, Blue Moon, 28 Years Later, It Was Just an Accident, Roofman, Sentimental Value, and Frankenstein!
5. My top 5:

1. Sentimental Value
2. Bugonia
3. One Battle After Another
4. Nouvelle Vague
5. Train Dreams

HMs: Rental Family, The Mastermind, Sorry Baby
6. Top new movies?
Sinners,
Fantastic 4,
Weapons,
F1,
Predator ( Badlands and Killer of Killers ),
HM: Bring her back
7. Probably the best from this year that I've seen are-

Sinners- excellent way to tie music history into a vampire period piece.

Bugonia- actually my favorite of Yorgos' work so far, less bombastic/cartoon-y than Poor Thing, tighter plot that still went pretty hard. 

Frankenstein- even beyond the gorgeous visual spectacle, surprisingly heartfelt and emotional. 

F1- did not think I was going to like this, ended up loving it. But I am an F1 fan so that probably influences my opinion. Just a really well done fun movie, don't have to think too hard. Days of Thunder vibes. 

One Battle After Another- just a really fun ride

Caught Stealing- I dunno how many people actually saw this film, but I love Aronofsky's movies and I love Austin Butler. I thought it was great, but probably not for everyone. Wild, and very violent.
8. The Life List (2025)

Relay (2025)
9. Except F1 I didn’t know a thing about any of these movies before seeing them. 

Sinners - loved the supernatural aspect, the cinematography, the score, the acting, the rich colors, the storyline

Weapons - loved the different point of views and Josh Brolin

Eternity - so, so funny, made me cry too, loved the production design

F1 - I still  like Brad Pitt, just felt like an epic sort of blockbuster about a subject I knew nothing about. Had no idea how much of a team was involved in car racing.  

The Perfect Neighbor (Netflix) - loved that it didn’t really have an outside narrator like a journalist and was almost all archival footage. It was very disturbing and moving and the actual movie just came out of left field for me. I was blown away. It was a very impactful way of story telling. 

I still have a lot of movies from 2025 that I haven’t seen (one battle after another, bugonia, Frankenstein) but this is what I have so far! :)

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. "The Fifth Element" was so far ahead of its time when it came out in 1997.

**Score:** 3276
**Body:** I still can't believe it came out in freaking 1997. I thought it was the coolest movie I had ever seen when I first saw it, and for quite a few years after. The special effects were crazy good for the time. The plot, characters, the cinematography, the editing, even the freaking dialogue were all top-notch. There are a few cheesy scenes looking back on it almost 30 years later, but still, I really feel that the movie was way, way ahead of its time. It did have a $90 million budget though, which was unbelievably massive for the time. That is probably why it looks so damn good and still holds up all this time later. It is also full of some classic lines. "Leeloo Dallas Multipass."

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I saw a video recently where a VFX guy made a video where he had 5 hours to create a shot vs a whole week.  The 5 hour shot actually ended up better because he used a miniature real life model and did some minor FX whereas the shot that took a week looked worse because it was entirely digital.  I think that's why it holds up is because VFX is at it's best when it's complimenting real elements (no pun intended), and you don't just shoot on a green screen and create everything from scratch.

On top of that, film actually looks good, not to rehash the tired film vs digital debate, but film still holds up.  I think digital has surpassed film if done well, but old movies from the 90s or earlier still look good because film has a high dynamic range, the highlights aren't blown out, and there is still detail in the shadows, and you don't have a color grade that's has crazy green/blue depressing or a hyper saturated grade that looks super bright and fake.

VFX vid:

[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=w3VTvobIsAk](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=w3VTvobIsAk)
  - In my humble opinion, 90s movies looked absolutely peak visually.
    - Just at that sweet spot where CG was still in its infancy, while practical effects still ruled. Even the most cost-cutting movie studio wasn't going to stake its career on unknown technology. So we have that one point in time where movies using CGI complemented special effects instead of replacing them.
2. Yeah it’s set in the future
  - r/angryupvote
3. Visual style was based on Moebius drawings from 80s.
  - It's also heavily influenced by Valerian and Laureline from Mezieres (who collaborated to the movie), which makes it basically a better Valerian adaptation than the official Valerian movie made by the same director.
  - Incal, yeah. A lot of Heavy Metal too.
4. I think part of its staying power is because it trusted the audience to fill in the gaps of the lore instead of beating us over the head with unnecessary exposition. 

One of the biggest problems with Sci-fi and Fantasy adaptation is that the script writers for some reason feel compelled to add all the "tell" from the books into the movie.

Fifth Element didn't need to tell us why Earth became a festering, dystopian hellhole. You could plug in anything that made sense to you, and you'd be right.

Same thing with a lot of other details. Like how we just see Plava La'guna(?) without some side character info dumping about her and her planet's. Or heck, how the stones ended up inside her.

So many movies try to hold your hand through the experience, and this one just plops what you need into your lap, and kicks you out of the plane to figure it out.
5. I unabashedly put this movie in my top 10 of all time and actually have a multipass tattoo

## 2. a funny actor mostly known for comedies who is surprisingly good in serious roles?

**Score:** 2933
**Body:** Steve Carell in Little Miss Sunshine (2006)

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Jim Carrey, everyone knows him for his over the top comedies, but Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind and The Truman Show show he can absolutely carry a serious role with real emotional depth
  - Even going full-method in Man on the Moon.
    - "No way... That's great! WE LANDED ON THE MOON!"
2. Both John Lithgow and Michael Keaton started out in comedy and surprised me with serious roles as villains.
3. Robin Williams
4. Carell is an absolute star at portraying serious characters
  - Foxcatcher
5. Tom Hanks used to fit this category, now he's known more for dramatic roles
6. Jason Bateman and Bob Odenkirk are both comedians that have put on some stellar serious acting roles.
7. Adam Sandler (Punch Drunk Love)

Jim Carrey (Eternal Sunshine)

Will Ferrell (Stranger Than Fiction)

Robin Williams (Good Will Hunting)

Melissa McCarthy (Can You Ever Forgive Me?)

## 3. Saddest character in movie history

**Score:** 1970
**Body:** He makes me cry everytime I watch Shawshank Redemption

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The world done got itself in a damn hurry. That line hits me hard everytime
  - The beautiful thing about this movie is that there are a handful of lines that could resonate in the 1940s the same as they might resonate today. This is surely one of them. Shawshank is one of my favorite movies ever because it's a meditation on life and what you choose to do with it, despite all the wrong turns and unexpected bullshit life heaps onto us.
2. Brooks was here.
  - So was Red.
3. Brooks was in prison for murdering his wife and daughter after a bad night at poker.  The movie wisely didn't bring this up.
  - Nope, they never did. Interesting aspect actually
4. Little foot realizing the shadow wasn't his mother and she really was gone
5. John Coffey in The Green Mile
  - I’m tired boss.
6. Lee Chandler “Manchester by the sea”

## 4. Share an example of an actor’s last role where they were still in their prime.

**Score:** 1441
**Body:** Just curious what some people think. Everyone can tell when an actor is no longer in their prime years, but for some its less obvious than others. I was watching Edge of Tomorrow the other day and it occurred to me that I was probably watching Tom Cruise in his last believable performance as a leading man in an intense action film. I love the Mission Impossible movies, but its a bit hard to watch at times now that he's in his 60's.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. McConaughey: Interstellar. He’s not washed, but that was the last of the McConaissance
2. Harrison Ford in Air Force One
  - Wow...went to imdb cuz I was like nahhh and that is a stunning drop off.
    - Blade Runner 2049 was really good imo, but I don’t recall Ford being a major component in its success.

The dude owned things in the 80s-90s, though.
      - The Fugitive fn rocks
3. Arnold in True Lies
4. I have yet to get tired of this movie.
5. I dont know if Christoph Waltz will ever top his two tarantino movies. Although He was unsurprisingly fantastic in Frankenstein but its no hans landa. Dont even get me started on spectre
6. He did Top Gun Maverick after this, completely believable
  - Maverick proved he still had it. Dude was outacting half the cast while pulling Gs like it was nothing.

## 5. Legendary but underrated comedy movie scenes

**Score:** 782
**Body:** Coming from EE myself i can never stop watching and laughing like crazy at this scene 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Glad these scenes were during the summer. I hear in the winter it gets really depressing
  - Miami wice! Number one new show!
2. An American nickel. I start my own hotel.
3. Nowhere. Near. Berlin!
  - Guys! He's going to Berlin.
4. Mi scusi by creepy Italian guy (credited same) is my favourite part, played by Fred Armisen.
5. BRATISLAVA !!! Can't believe he made out with his sister but hey stop hammer time.
  - Is there a train coming any time soon? Oh yes, very soon. They are building it now
6. The disturbing little brother scene had me laughing so hard I couldn’t breathe. 🙋‍♂️
7. The movie is Euro Trip in case anyone was wondering

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Frankenstein (2025) was such a disappointment

**Score:** 264
**Body:** SPOILERS BELOW!!!!

God, watching this movie legitimately made me sad. Not because it moved me but because it was such an  horrible adaptation of such a wonderful novel. I imagine if you've never read Frankenstein, you would definitely find some enjoyment out of it, but as someone who loves the book, I couldn't help but think after watching it that this movie was such a simplistic and one-dimensional retelling of a far better story.

The problem really arises from the simple black-and-white view the movie takes on Victor and his creation. I mean, the movie paints Victor as a manipulative and abusive creep. The book has none of that yet paints a picture of a far more compelling monster. Victor is a monster because he is so self-centered and self-absorbed to the point where he has little to no consideration for others.

When he first creates his monster, he doesn't start abusing him but rather runs away in horror of what he's done, giving no consideration for what may become of his creation. Later he discovers that his loved ones have been worried sick for him, as he basically hadn't told a soul what he'd been up to or where he'd been for months. When the monster tells Victor that he will see him again upon his wedding night, right after Victor destroys the female companion intended for his creation, he only thinks that he might be in danger rather than considering that the monster would go after Elizabeth. Victor in the books is a grey character, not malicious in intent or spirit but rather just a man whose carelessness and selfishness inflict so much pain on those around him.

The Monster as well is a far more shallow version than the monster presented by Shelley, and make no mistake, in the books he is a Monster. He may not have been born a monster, true, but he very much becomes one. Del Toro, however, just transforms the monster into a misunderstood victim, and in doing so, makes him lose all his agency and nuance. In a strange way this movie sort of reminds me of those Disney villain origin movies. Where you take a villainous figure and go, "Oh, here's the REAL story," but in so doing just make the character lose everything that made them interesting and engaging.

In the book the monster is a person that craves companionship, love, and affection from the others, yet he has been brought into a world that is revolted by him. People look at him only with disgust and horror, and when faced with such a reality, he lashes out at those who have hurt him, comforting himself with that at least he can inflict pain on others in return. But in the movie, he is just a poor misunderstood baby.

You can clearly see this in how the movie does in how it deals with the blind old man. In the movie the blind man gets killed by a couple of wolves; some guys find the blind man with the monster and attack him in the belief that the monster killed him. Just a poor misunderstood victim. In the book, the monster is discovered by the family living at the house, who are horrified by him and chase him away, and when the monster returns, the family has left the house. When faced with being rejected once more by people he wanted to get close to, he proceeds to burn the whole house down in anger and revenge.

The moment the movie lost me, where I couldn't bear to watch anymore, was right about the moment where Victor killed Elizabeth because it was just awful in every imaginable way. The whole notion that Elizabeth cares/has a thing for the monster just ruins the relationship between Victor and the monster and the tragedy that Victor inflicted on his creation. In the book, Elizabeth is Victor's love, whom the monster kills, along with causing the death, either directly or indirectly, of everyone Victor loves, to condemn his creator to the same fate that Victor brought upon him: to live an existence devoid of the love of others.

And with the movie establishing that Elizabeth has a thing for the monster, his tragedy no longer becomes "I live under the unbearable burden of having been created as a violation of nature, thus having no place in God's design," but rather "Oh, my daddy was mean to me and shot my crush." Like, why are you so upset, monster? You don't exist under the terrible weight of knowing that no one in the world can ever love someone like you. You just need to find another freak like Mia Goth who wants to climb you like a mountain, and everything will be alright.

Having watched this movie, I can come to no other conclusion than that Del Toro was definitely the wrong person to make this movie. Del Toro loves monsters and as such cannot see Frankenstein's monster as monstrous. He isn't a result of man's ambition gone too far; he's just "misunderstood." The movie looks great; it is well-acted and well-made. But everything about the execution of the story so utterly pales in comparison to Shelley's masterpiece that I can honestly say that this movie is the most disappointing thing I have ever seen.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. While I'm not a fan of the book particularly (having to read it many times as an A-Level settext will kill love for any book), I was also disappointed that the complexity of Victor and the Monster was stripped away. I actually found the Monster more of a tragic figure in the book because of that complexity, because of the way the world twists him into the monster they thought he was born as.


And as you said, Victor is not great person, not a likable person, but there's all sorts of layers of greyness in the book that I wish Del Toro had played with more. And the book allows for all sorts of fun readings generally. I remember some book critics enjoying how Victor tries to supplant the role of women (childbirth) and that Elizabeth's death on their wedding night before the marriage is consummated can be read as Victor's fear of women and sex.


I loved the prototype monsters and the over-the-top sets and costumes. But it was a dolls house - because the characters were so flat. I love Pan's Labyrinth very much (it's a top ten film for me) but the Good vs Evil worldview in that film fits the fairytale themes of the story. (That, and The Faun himself is an ambivalent figure - we are at turns frightened of him and comforted by him. So Del Toro can create ambiguous monsters!). In Del Toro's more recent works, the lack of complexity in his characters, the huge neon arrows pointing at the Baddies vs the Goodies really puts me off (I loathed The Shape of Water).


What also really ticked me off was the Byron quote at the end. Of course Byron is involved in the history of the novel. But that the most influential science fiction novel of all time was written by a young woman (and the daughter of a feminist writer!) does feel important to me and for all of Byron's talents, he very much feels like the antithesis of all that.


I feel bad for being so critical because GDT is such a lovely man and so passionate about films. But his last few films have not been for me.
  - the byron quote felt SO weird to me
2. I think the source material is very personal to Del Toro and what he carries with him is the relationships between the monster and its creator and the world around it. I think that's the movie we have. I don't agree with expecting him to faithfully adapt the book, but rather I would say we should explore what he's saying in his adaptation.

Alternatively, I believe he said it's not at AI parable, but it kind of works as one! Megalomaniac tech bro creates AI to feed his ego, gets trained on Reddit shit posts, and goes sideways.
  - I don't really expect an adaptation to be 'faithful' to the original as a unique take is what make adaptations interesting. However I do think Del Toro stripped the original of its nuance and did not replace it with anything particularly substantial. *That* makes it a poor adaptation, in my opinion.
3. My issue with Del Toro's last two monster flicks is that I find their anthropocentrism really boring. The monster in Frankenstein hints in one scene that he has the memories and maybe spirits of the dead men he's composed of inside him. Instead of getting an exploration of how an intelligence like that might differ from our own we get "oh, it's actually not that different, all you need is love." In Shape of Water, same deal. The creature is only as alien as a blind date who hasn't arrived to dinner yet.
4. I largely agree, I thought the movie was fine (especially production/costume design) but Del Toro swapped out a lot of the elements that made the book interesting (mostly the nuance of Viktor and the creature) for things he likes (sympathetic mosters).


I could get on board with the more fairy-tale logic and aesthetic of the movie, but I just didn't feel like it was worth it.


I mean, if the creature upon coming alive is first met with love from both Victor and Mia Goth, we're already dealing with a radically different story than Mary Shelley's novel. And all for daddy issues and "my sexy corpse boyfriend"?


And sure, have it be a daddy issues movie, but GDT then immediately has Victor and the monster resolve their issues in the final scene without any clear reason?
  - I feel like this movie would have gone over better had it been inspired by frankenstein rather than ostensibly an adaptation. I’m reminded of the Coen bros O Brother Where Art Thou which was inspired by the odyssey but wasn’t a strict adaptation so they could play with the elements they wanted without being bound by the existing story. 

There was a lot to like about del Toro’s movie but his choice to make the monster a sexy dude who’s just an innocent victim looking for love doesn’t make a lot of sense.
5. I personally was baffled by the choice to have the creature brutally massacre a number of sailors, and then an hour later hard cut to him talking about how chill he is. 


It seemed really disjointed.


It's also strange that Victor's telling of the event didn't paint him at all as a good person. He comes across as such a short sighted, abusive, ass (with a stupid hat) that it's a wonder the captain has anything but pity for the creature when it finally breaks its way into the captain's quarters to tell its side of the story.


If you're doing a yojimbo, painting one character as unsympathetically as possible during their own story is bizarre. 


Like every Del Toro movie, there is a lot of beautiful imagery and striking father-son melodrama, but 
... Shit, Netflix freaking marketed this movie with the tagline "Only monsters play God." He's not doing a whole lot of messing about with subtext.


I dunno, after watching it, I sort of felt like Del Toro's intent in making Frankenstein was to make an art house movie for idiots who aren't paying attention to any scene that isn't a spectacle. It's like he had no faith the audience would understand the message of the story without spoon feeding it to us.
6. Ever seen any of the Hammer Frankenstein movies? 


Peter Cushing is even more villainous. He finds an old professor and murders him to harvest the brain BEFORE the monster is even animated.


He is not only cheating on Elizabeth, he has his affair partner killed by the monster.


And his carelessness leads to a situation where he shoots Elizabeth.


You don't have to love the direction, but your critiques are not new to Frankenstein adaptations.
  - The Hammer Frankensteins are so great

## 2. A Review of 'Train Dreams' (2025)

**Score:** 23
**Body:** ***“It was only when you left it alone that a tree might treat you as a friend. After the blade bit in, you had yourself a war.”***

I found 'Train Dreams' to be very moving. There have been whispers and, slowly, shouts of ''hollow'' attempts to pastiche Terrence Malick forming, or comparisons to 'The Assassination of Jesse James by the Coward Robert Ford', and claims that Will Patton's narration often does nothing but dilute silent scenes into explanations; with all that said and acknowledged, for better or worse, this film moved me. At the end of the day, it features the profoundly performed interiority of Robert Grainier—a man who lived most of his life in a perpetual reckoning between pain and pleasure—and, with all that said, it stars the truly underrated Joel Edgerton, a filmmaker who can act with such ferocity in his eyes and direct and write with the rare touch of potential we sometimes see in actors. Edgerton has been an almost ''must-watch'' actor for me for what has been a few years now; he is making worthwhile—at the very least interesting—choices as a performer.

There are a few performances in this film that really struck me. As has been mentioned many a time, William H. Macy's character, Arn, is a wonderfully endearing presence and source of comic relief as he plays a still-working old-timer who imparts every aspect of his knowledge unsolicited and ends up dying from the disorderly trauma of a falling branch. His line about always having a family ''everywhere there is a smiling face'' ends up utterly vanquishing that initial impression that he is lonely; here is a man who knows his place in humanity. Of course, there is the aforementioned Edgerton as the tender, ruptured protagonist who wears a face we can see is at all times split between his present surroundings in the fodder forest or the railroad and the pastoral comforts of his small family's warm log cabin lit by oil lamps like a van Schendel painting. Edgerton has been an intricate performer for as long as I have seen him on screen. He deserves his plaudits, and I hope that he continues to be highlighted by films like this.

Two other performances deserve to be talked of: Nathaniel Arcand's subtle scene-stealing as Ignatius Jack, a Native American friend of Grainier who is a shopkeeper and serves as a boon to grieving Grainier in silent but steady ways. Arcand does a fantastic job of capturing and encapsulating the essence of compassion in his taciturn character's expressions. Out of the woodland blaze is reborn the log cabin in its second form with the succour of Ignatius Jack. Secondly, the performance of Felicity Jones as Gladys, Robert's wife, effectively showcases exactly what she meant to him and how much. Gladys is intelligent, resilient, has a playful sense of humour, is affectionate, and is honest. His loss of her strips him of a true partner and scars any part of him that considers moving on. Even his life with the pups that become his dogs is marked by a conversation they once had and an idea Gladys articulated—the fact puppies know more than babies. It is a terribly tragic turn of events to witness, knowing just how well they worked together. I can hardly say anything about the loss of his two-year-old daughter, Kate.

Technically, Clint Bentley's film has a very idiosyncratic execution. You have the, as previously mentioned, narration of Will Patton (who also narrated the novella), which I did not mind, but it is absolutely fair to say does sometimes explicate too much of Grainier's interior mind; also, this narration is certainly redolent of 'The Assassination of Jesse James by the Coward Ford's poetic voiceover. There is the Malickian focus on liquid camera movement and time-traversing, but this film stands alone enough in Adolpho Veloso's cinematography to not owe any particularly large debts. The pacing of the final third could have also been a little more economical; more elaboration of Grainier's elderly years might have been a compelling decision.

I was often reminded of Kelly Reichardt's 'First Cow' whilst watching this; there is a particular focus on the lush soundscape of the forest, the slowness of the story, the birth of a home and the American frontier, intermittent visual focus from a distance, and the microcosmic diversity of characters that makes this comparison work well, to my mind. Then there is Bryce Dessner's elegiac score. This score is nothing short of poignantly moving when it is deployed. It is very simple in its traversals, but the leitmotif of its main track, 'Passageways I: Ahead, Trembling', had a solemnity I consistently appreciated. Nick Cave's contribution to the song 'Train Dreams' for the credits was my favourite musical feature. 

I have not read the novella this film is based on, so I cannot speak to fidelity, the value of directorial choices, and its translation to the cinematic screen, but I can say that there is a roving quality to this film that I often hope for in films; there is a persistent undercurrent in the story's twists and turns to quantify ''life''—to work out whether time at this job is worth it, whether this amount of money is enough to quit it for, whether the time spent with a daughter who drastically transforms each time you see her is ever going to be enough—but, with that ending, its themes simply ask us to think qualitatively about our lives; to consider and reckon with our trajectories with more appreciation.

One of the larger ideas I gleaned from the film was transformation. The way the world shapes and forms itself as glaciers melt, as trees are cut down, as the forest is sparked by a flame and cannibalises itself, as human bodies die and become one with the soil; as human lives are changed by marriage, death, childbirth, child and pet rearing, the physical labour of work, and the traumatic imprints of seeing a man killed probably for the colour of his skin; the way civilisation transforms in a lifetime—in this particular lifetime ranging from the fin de siècle to the moon landing—the birth of images and sounds on screens, the death of railroads or bridges in lieu of motorways and cars, and the prices we are paid for sometimes soul-destroying work and then end up paying down the line to fly. It is always changing, but there is invariably something of beauty to be found, somewhere.

Robert Grainier, an orphan, widower, and bereaved parent, has lost everything at every stage of his life, except the end. Throughout his life, he remains a soft-spoken, kindly man who deserved more than what happened to him. In the beginning, he loses his immediate family; in the middle, he works as a railroad construction man and then as a seasonal logger at $4 a day around 1920—which is to his purse's credit but is never anything more than a cost given the time spent away from his wife and daughter. At the end, flying like a bird on that $4 biplane ride in 1968, he gains some closing appreciation for it all as he thinks back on the touch of his wife, the face of his baby daughter, her voice when she only grew up to be a toddler, the generosity of a Native American friend, and the wisdom of a lonesome old feller.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It is a fine film for the cinematography, acting, and quiet atmosphere. The script and choice to (over)narrate is perhaps not equally good - there was even a touch of preachiness at times, and a sense that the scope of subject matter was too wide. This can be disputed, I guess, as it was about a life and perhaps the point was that any life is bound up with the whole variety of the issues that exist in the world. I too was reminded of "First Cow" and also films like "Without a Trace" with a similar atmosphere.

I'm not so sure that the cinematography isn't heavily referential, if not derivative. The use of water and fire and the brief flashes of memory could have come directly from Tarkovsky's "Mirror", while the scenes with the husband and wife in the great outdoors, especially a shot with them lying in the grass, must have been closely based on Malick's "A Hidden Life". I think these were not done in imitation alone, but are an expression that the film at its heart is tackling issues related to the ones in Malick and Tarkovsky - this man's life, as much as any half-forgotten martyr of Nazism whose resistance was not in any way influential, was truly a "hidden life", yet he made his own contribution and we are given a glimpse into an interior life that has its own worth regardless of what he is paid for his labour or how many will remember him.

I, for one, certainly don't object to this film wearing its heritage on its sleeve. There are elements in this which I don't see how they can be improved (the same is true of the overall much more flawed Del Toro "Frankenstein", which sometimes had almost the Platonic visual form of the objects and landscapes it was trying to tackle), and it makes me feel much better about Netflix.
  - Yes, it is entirely fair to say that the cinematography is referential. I also noticed the similarities in that shot with 'A Hidden Life'. But then again, it is a couple lying on grass. I will say that any film set in the twentieth century and focusing on the rustic life of a man in the woods is going to end up being quite evocative if every other film or story that has attempted that. The question should be whether it was done well, and though there are certainly flaws, visual clichés, or directorial misfires, it is a moving film.

As for Netflix, I think their connection to this only goes as far as acquiring it for distribution but it is a step in the right direction.
2. It sounds like an interesting film. I haven't read much about it, or even seen the trailer, but from your description, and other descriptions of the film, it sounds remarkably like Ein ganzes Leben, an Austrian film with a very similar story and focus on the land and setting.

The 2023 film is one of a man who comes of age at the start of the 20th century in a deep Alpen valley, lives through almost the same personal tragedy, and lives a simple, physical life as the world around him modernizes and changes. Even the beats with the friend dying in an unusual and unfortunate way run parallel. It's based on a book of the same name by Robert Seethaler. I wonder if that book was inspired by Train Dreams, or if it's just a matter of coincidence.

I will say that the novel you mentioned seems a little more inclined towards violence/graphic depictions, if my assumptions are correct? 'Train Dreams' mutes itself in that regard.

## 3. Do you think movies are “overproduced” nowdays? Is that even a thing?

**Score:** 26
**Body:** I was pondering about how movies nowadays are looking quite uglier than before, they feel more fake, even if the scenery is real or the movie is of very high production. a good example is the Frankenstein del toro movie, the Ship stranded on the ice is real yet feels like a cheap green screen at times. Some people believe this is because cameras are just “getting too good” but I dont think that’s enough

the only conclusion I have come up is that movies are becoming too overproduced, convulted with elements that arent necessary.

for example color correction has become more extreme. The way Sunlight hit the actors face looks fake with soft shadows and unnatural balanced light, with no room for over exposure or under exposure, like they are in a photography session. I have noticed a more common use of distorted lenses, always some kind of distortion here and there just because. A lot of extreme defocusing of the background

what are your thought?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I'm not sure overproduced is the right word, but I do think everything is a bit too perfect now. If you look at even the best masterpieces of cinema from before digital of any sort including editing, you'll find many mistakes. Eg. A slightly wobbly camera move, or marks on the film, or unintentional lens flares. 

I think now with things so perfect and over edited you get something that just feels artificial or unrealistic. Back before digital effects everything had to be real. Camera movements had to be possible. And it just gave a grounded feeling, especially to things like genre cinema. 

So, I'd say films are too perfect these days.
  - I think it's the opposite. They are under produced and shot with bland cinematography.

The variation in the look,feel of movies of the 70s varied so much from my movie to movie. I know the shoting stock of different film types 

So many movies now you could grab a random frame and you wouldn't be able to tell the difference between movies.

The technology might lean towards that flat look but it's up to the director to push against that technology. 

The technology might lean that way but
  - 'you get something that just feels artificial or unrealistic'

which is ironic because i'd argue that a wobbly camera and unintended lens flare are obviously artificial and unrealistic!

but nevertheless, it's accurate today that films are so polished now that they no longer feel  tangible. and perhaps tangibility was something that we valued that's been lost now.
  - I think it also goes beyond the digital aspect but it is a huge part of it. Everything feels lit the same way. Costumes and makeup don't feel real anymore because they are able to just fix something in post with CGI. This isn't every new movie, but it is a lot of major releases.
    - I think theres a lot of independent and world cinema that doesn't feel like this, but Hollywood and blockbusters definitely do.
2. I would say that *Wicked* was a perfect example of this. I enjoyed it (haven't seen the second yet), but it was bloated, overlong, overstuffed, overproduced, and so much of it was so wasted on the screen!

Like, there's so much effort in the production design, sets, and costumes, but it's badly and flatly lit and then drenched in so much unnecessary CGI that frankly makes the colors sort of drab when they should be brilliant.

The CGI in that movie is just such overkill and it makes so much of it feel slightly out of focus and kind of ugly, when Oz in the original movie was just so stunning -- 90 years ago.

Again, I did enjoy the first movie, but oh my God, they should have cut 45 minutes and could have done so without missing a blink, and the whole thing should've been one movie.

I still loved the performances and a lot of the music was great. But it's kind of sad that they spent so much money and it looks so weirdly pallid and lifeless.
3. My old-man-yells-at-cloud opinion about this is that cameras are MUCH better in low light with a ton of dynamic range now, but that has made lighting absolutely worse. The lighting strategy for the digital world has been to shoot for a flat malleable raw image which you can then blast with color in post where you can make all sorts of minute decisions, as opposed to being intentional and lighting the entire frame more or less with what you want and thinking of contrast ratios and color saturation in camera.

In the old days of film you were FAR more limited in your post color options so you had to do a lot of planning ahead to "get it in camera," and light for contrast, knowing that your blacks would only look velvety and lovely if you lit for them that way and let them recede into the abyss. Now cinematographers are terrified to let ANYTHING get clipped or crushed because that material is technically unworkable in post, but if you intended for the thing to be that dark then you might as well let it fall off and live with it.

Anyways, what this means is that films have a LOT less contrast than they used to. Everything is much flatter, and contrast comes instead from color choices, but then again, a lot of films choose to use one main color and then keep everything in that shade, and that color is usually some green/beige/brown. They even have a name for it: [sludge](https://www.dailymail.co.uk/lifestyle/article-10394673/How-cinema-went-bright-hues-intangible-sludge.html). That article I'm linking to has some good examples of how shows used to be lit and colored vs how they are lit and colored today.

Anyway, there are still really great movies being made that don't do this - the one I think of off the top of my head is "Knives Out" which has absolutely wonderful lighting and contrast, and that DP Steve Yedlin is a true master of the craft and comfortable with both film and digital. Which raises the point that if Yedlin can do these amazing dynamic looks with digital tools, why can't everyone else?

And the answer is that cinematography is really hard and there are a lot of people in the industry who aren't very good at it, and they're the ones making the sludge, barely lighting it, getting it colored, and continuing to get work because they're a "safe" pic for streamers like Netflix, which have VERY strict standards for how their libraries ought to look and keep lists of cinematographers they approve of, and the best way to get on that list is to not rock the boat.

Making choices is the one thing no one actually wants to do in the industry. They want to keep the maximal  range of options open as long as possible because if it turns out bad maybe you can get it through by changing something in post instead of committing to a vision now. Better not to have a vision and hopefully find it later.
4. I definitely found Frankenstein to both look good and bad at the same time. Del Toro is always great at art direction but I agree on parts looking a little too fake. The thing that really bothered me when watching it though, was the camera work. Theres some really great shots but I’m not sure there’s a shot where the camera is still, it’s always slightly moving and it drove me crazy.
5. It sounds and seems like we drifted toward what Umberto Eco described as hyperreal. In his musings he astutely describes the situation where things created in a way to be 'more than real', perfected beyond what the corresponding real things are, are becoming fake - but we appreciate them more than the real things.

Travels in Hyperreality (and his Faith in Fakes) are great essay collections for this very phenomenon - we have now CGI-'perfected' camera footage and over-tweaked, over-perfected shots that become the norm and we end up treating them as the normal - then we feel that we lack something when we see the real thing.

Just few weeks ago on some film subreddit (can't recall which) some ranted about how old films looked 'bad'... perfect illustration how things have turned upside down.
6. Yes, they often are overproduced, especially blockbuster type movies. I love when the camera just sits and attends what’s happening, even when not much is happening and it slowly unfolds. When editing, music, and special effects aren’t heavy handed and manipulative and leading the audience by the hand. Of course, there are many great films with strong production values that are gorgeous and many great ones with a strongly presented POV. All I’m saying is simple can be very compelling. And that you can make a movie feel dumber and less interesting with too heavy a hand

## 4. Does anyone have any in-depth readings about the length of feature films?

**Score:** 5
**Body:** Nowadays, a "movie" is supposed to be anywhere between 80 minutes and 4 hours. Anything outside of that and people won't want to watch it in a theater.

How did we get into this window of runtimes? It seems like early on, they were really unsure of how long a film should be. Von Stroheim's Greed (1928) had a 9 hour runtime, Gance' Napoleon is 7+ hours, Fritz Lang has multiple 4 hour silent films but on the other hand, you have many silents that are in the ~60 minute range.


Were people actually expected to sit in a theater a day watch 9 hour films? Were they works of art meant to be showed to high society? Were people mad if they paid for admission and only saw a 60 minute film?

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Those movies were outliers and considered events. Plus, *Greed* was cut down to 140 minutes for release. *Napoleon* was cut to 111 by its U.S. distributor. Fritz Lang’s longest movies were released in two parts.

Lengthy movies are still being made. Olivier Assayas’ *Carlos* is 339 minutes. *Nymphomaniac* is 325 minutes. *Che* is 269 minutes. All of the above were shown in theaters with those running times. 

Shorter features in the Hollywood studio era were very common. They were made to be part of a double bill and were called B-movies.
  - This. 

Back in the day, for the Silent Era through the 50s and 60s when television was becoming more prevalent, you would go to the theater expecting an entire evening or afternoon of entertainment. Most people went to the movies once a week. While a lot of people had radios, visual entertainment in the form of TV wouldn't enter most home until about  65-70 years ago.

At that time you usually didn't just watch a single feature film. Theaters would continuously run a sleight of programs throughout the day and evening. There would be newsreels, short subjects (that's where you got stuff like The Three Stooges), and cartoons running in between the latest popular movies that week, the A-movie, as well as shorter feature films that showed alongside them, the B-movies.

It was a lot closer to what broadcast television or streaming is today, and like regular TV viewers do today you might just chill out and watch a couple movies for a few hours.
2. I will add to the other answer that the probable reason 80 minutes to 4 hours became the norm, is because it is a reasonable runtime boundary for watching a movie in a single sitting. Or more particularly watching a movie in a single sitting in theaters.

## 5. Aftersun: Greatest Ending Ever?

**Score:** 5
**Body:** Yerp, I know claiming the 'greatest' of anything in the realm of art is somewhat ridiculous as art is subjective. Furthermore, I would never feel in any position to claim that anything is 'the best of all time' as there are so many variables and different aspects that one might find great about a paritcular film, while films themselves are fundamentally different and a comparison can feel like the equivalent of comparing apples and oranges. 

With all that being said, I think the ending of Aftersun, in paritcular the montage set to Mercury and Bowie's *Under Pressure* and how this montage perfectly captures the devasting effects of generational trauma and the disengration of a father and daughter's relationship, might have a case for being the greatest ending ever of any film. I don't necessarily think that this a controversial or edgy take as I can't quite remember being as emotional devastated from a film like Aftersun.

That’s actually my least favorite movie from Antonioni’s trilogy but the ending itself is quite striking. It’s like a piece of music, but composed from image and visual texture. There actually is some accompanying music, but overall the ending is rather silent. It’s a formally exciting montage.
